text: A century after its discovery Fischer–Tropsch synthesis (FTS) remains one of the most studied processes in catalysis both scientifically and industrially. During the last decade the FTS field has experienced a paradigm shift in terms of the focus from macroscopic studies to molecular level descriptions of the process using complementary surface science, density functional theory (DFT) and microkinetic modelling approaches. This has allowed for deeper understanding of the cobalt based FTS catalysts such as describing the structure of the catalytically active nanoparticle and the characteristics of the stable CO overlayers formed on them. It has also allowed for consensus on the catalytic sites on such catalysts such as the role of terraces versus edges and carbide versus CO insertion mechanisms. In contrast iron-based catalysts remain to be fully understood at the molecular level due to their complexity which stems from the dynamic operation of a multitude of oxide and carbide species under FTS conditions. Although considerable progress has been reported in understanding the stability of the iron species present under FTS and the effect of promoters on them, a detailed "movie" of the molecular catalytic site is not yet at the hands. This is also due to the emerging interest in the area of CO2-FTS and the use of long chain hydrocarbons as feedstocks. Understanding the molecular level concepts derived from studying the process of CO hydrogenation can shed the light towards further advancement of the carbon utilisation technology and the CCU process in general.
explanation: High domain-specific coherence and a polished scholarly tone are consistent with AI or expert human writing. A few unusual phrasings (e.g., 'shed the light towards') hint at human editing, keeping the text plausibly human-produced but not definitively so.
